book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
Concerning the Unity of God. On the Article, I Believe in One God. Also Concerning Heresies.

ccel.title: NPNF2-07. Cyril of Jerusalem, Gregory Nazianzen

source.title: NPNF2-07. Cyril of Jerusalem, Gregory Nazianzen

title: Concerning the Unity of God. On the Article, I Believe in One God. Also Concerning Heresies.

source.title: NPNF2-07. Cyril of Jerusalem, Gregory Nazianzen

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Sanctify yourselves unto Me, O islands.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Israel is saved by the Lord with an everlasting salvation; they shall not be ashamed, neither shall they be confounded for ever, &c.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Blessed also be His Only-begotten Son.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For with the thought of God let the thought of Father at once be joined, that the ascription of glory to the Father and the Son may be made indivisible.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For the Father hath not one glory, and the Son another, but one and the same, since He is the Father’s Only-begotten Son; and when the Father is glorified, the Son also shares the glory with Him, because the glory of the Son flows from His Father’s honour: and again, when the Son is glorified, the Father of so great a blessing is highly honoured.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Now though the mind is most rapid in its thoughts, yet the tongue needs words, and a long recital of intermediary speech.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For the eye embraces at once a multitude of the ‘starry quire;’ but when any one wishes to describe them one by one, which is the Morning-star, and which, the Evening-star, and which each one of them, he has need of many words.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: In like manner again the mind in the briefest moment compasses earth and sea and all the bounds of the universe; but what it conceives in an instant, it uses many words to describe.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Yet forcible as is the example I have mentioned, still it is after all weak and inadequate.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For of God we speak not all we ought (for that is known to Him only), but so much as the capacity of human nature has received, and so much as our weakness can bear.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For we explain not what God is but candidly confess that we have not exact knowledge concerning Him.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For in what concerns God to confess our ignorance is the best knowledge.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Therefore magnify the Lord with me, and let us exalt His Name together,—all of us in common, for one alone is powerless; nay rather, even if we be all united together, we shall yet not do it as we ought.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I mean not you only who are here present, but even if all the nurslings of the whole Church throughout the world, both that which now is and that which shall be, should meet together, they would not be able worthily to sing the praises of their Shepherd.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: A great and honourable man was Abraham, but only great in comparison with men; and when he came before God, then speaking the truth candidly he saith, I am earth and ashes.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He did not say ‘earth,’ and then cease, lest he should call himself by the name of that great element; but he added ‘and ashes,’ that he might represent his perishable and frail nature.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Is there anything, he saith, smaller or lighter than ashes?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For take, saith he, the comparison of ashes to a house, of a house to a city, a city to a province, a province to the Roman Empire, and the Roman Empire to the whole earth and all its bounds, and the whole earth to the heaven in which it is embosomed;—the earth, which bears the same proportion to the heaven as the centre to the whole circumference of a wheel, for the earth is no more than this in comparison with the heaven: consider then that this first heaven which is seen is less than the second, and the second than the third, for so far Scripture has named them, not that they are only so many, but because it was expedient for us to know so many only.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And when in thought thou hast surveyed all the heavens, not yet will even the heavens be able to praise God as He is, nay, not if they should resound with a voice louder than thunder.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But if these great vaults of the heavens cannot worthily sing God’s praise, when shall ‘earth and ashes,’ the smallest and least of things existing, be able to send up a worthy hymn of praise to God, or worthily to speak of God, that sitteth upon the circle of the earth, and holdeth the inhabitants thereof as grasshoppers.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If any man attempt to speak of God, let him first describe the bounds of the earth.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Thou dwellest on the earth, and the limit of this earth which is thy dwelling thou knowest not: how then shalt thou be able to form a worthy thought of its Creator?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Thou beholdest the stars, but their Maker thou beholdest not: count these which are visible, and then describe Him who is invisible, Who telleth the number of the stars, and calleth them all by their names.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Violent rains lately came pouring down upon us, and nearly destroyed us: number the drops in this city alone: nay, I say not in the city, but number the drops on thine own house for one single hour, if thou canst: but thou canst not.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Learn then thine own weakness; learn from this instance the mightiness of God: for He hath numbered the drops of rain, which have been poured down on all the earth, not only now but in all time.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The sun is a work of God, which, great though it be, is but a spot in comparison with the whole heaven; first gaze stedfastly upon the sun, and then curiously scan the Lord of the sun.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Seek not the things that are too deep for thee, neither search out the things that are above thy strength: what is commanded thee, think thereupon.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But some one will say, If the Divine substance is incomprehensible, why then dost thou discourse of these things?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: So then, because I cannot drink up all the river, am I not even to take in moderation what is expedient for me?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Because with eyes so constituted as mine I cannot take in all the sun, am I not even to look upon him enough to satisfy my wants?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Or again, because I have entered into a great garden, and cannot eat all the supply of fruits, wouldst thou have me go away altogether hungry?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I praise and glorify Him that made us; for it is a divine command which saith, Let every breath praise the Lord.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I am attempting now to glorify the Lord, but not to describe Him, knowing nevertheless that I shall fall short of glorifying Him worthily, yet deeming it a work of piety even to attempt it at all.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For the Lord Jesus encourageth my weakness, by saying, No man hath seen God at any time.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: What then, some man will say, is it not written, The little ones’ Angels do always behold the face of My Father which is in heaven?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Yes, but the Angels see God not as He is, but as far as they themselves are capable.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For it is Jesus Himself who saith, Not that any man hath seen the Father, save He which is of God, He hath seen the Father.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The Angels therefore behold as much as they can bear, and Archangels as much as they are able; and Thrones and Dominions more than the former, but yet less than His worthiness: for with the Son the Holy Ghost alone can rightly behold Him: for He searcheth all things, and knoweth even the deep things of God: as indeed the Only-begotten Son also, with the Holy Ghost, knoweth the Father fully: For neither, saith He, knoweth any man the Father, save the Son, and he to whom the Son will reveal Him.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For He fully beholdeth, and, according as each can bear, revealeth God through the Spirit: since the Only-begotten Son together with the Holy Ghost is a partaker of the Father’s Godhead.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He, who was begotten knoweth Him who begat; and He Who begat knoweth Him who is begotten.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Since Angels then are ignorant (for to each according to his own capacity doth the Only-begotten reveal Him through the Holy Ghost, as we have said), let no man be ashamed to confess his ignorance.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I am speaking now, as all do on occasion: but how we speak, we cannot tell: how then can I declare Him who hath given us speech?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I who have a soul, and cannot tell its distinctive properties, how shall I be able to describe its Giver?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For devotion it suffices us simply to know that we have a God; a God who is One, a living, an ever-living God; always like unto Himself; who has no Father, none mightier than Himself, no successor to thrust Him out from His kingdom: Who in name is manifold, in power infinite, in substance uniform.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For though He is called Good, and Just, and Almighty and Sabaoth, He is not on that account diverse and various; but being one and the same, He sends forth countless operations of His Godhead, not exceeding here and deficient there, but being in all things like unto Himself.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Not great in loving-kindness only, and little in wisdom, but with wisdom and loving-kindness in equal power: not seeing in part, and in part devoid of sight; but being all eye, and all ear, and all mind: not like us perceiving in part and in part not knowing; for such a statement were blasphemous, and unworthy of the Divine substance.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He foreknoweth the things that be; He is Holy, and Almighty, and excelleth all in goodness, and majesty, and wisdom: of Whom we can declare neither beginning, nor form, nor shape.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For ye have neither heard His voice at any time, nor seen His shape, saith Holy Scripture.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Wherefore Moses saith also to the Israelites: And take ye good heed to your own souls, for ye saw no similitude.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For if it is wholly impossible to imagine His likeness, how shall thought come near His substance?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: There have been many imaginations by many persons, and all have failed.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Some have thought that God is fire; others that He is, as it were, a man with wings, because of a true text ill understood, Thou shalt hide me under the shadow of Thy wings.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: They forgot that our Lord Jesus Christ, the Only-begotten, speaks in like manner concerning Himself to Jerusalem, How often would I have gathered thy children together even as a hen doth gather her chickens under her wings, and ye would not.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For whereas God’s protecting power was conceived as wings, they failing to understand this sank down to the level of things human, and supposed that the Unsearchable exists in the likeness of man.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Some again dared to say that He has seven eyes, because it is written, seven eyes of the Lord looking upon the whole earth.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For if He has but seven eyes surrounding Him in part, His seeing is therefore partial and not perfect: but to say this of God is blasphemous; for we must believe that God is in all things perfect, according to our Saviour’s word, which saith, Your Father in heaven is perfect: perfect in sight, perfect in power, perfect in greatness, perfect in foreknowledge, perfect in goodness, perfect in justice, perfect in loving-kindness: not circumscribed in any space, but the Creator of all space, existing in all, and circumscribed by none.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Heaven is His throne, but higher is He that sitteth thereon: and earth is His footstool, but His power reacheth unto things under the earth.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: One He is, everywhere present, beholding all things, perceiving all things, creating all things through Christ: For all things were made by Him, and without Him was not anything made.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: A fountain of every good, abundant and unfailing, a river of blessings, an eternal light of never-failing splendour, an insuperable power condescending to our infirmities: whose very Name we dare not hear.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Wilt thou find a footstep of the Lord? saith Job, or hast thou attained unto the least things which the Almighty hath made?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If the least of His works are incomprehensible, shall He be comprehended who made them all?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Eye hath not seen, and ear hath not heard, neither have entered into the heart of man, the things which God hath prepared for them that love Him.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If the things which God hath prepared are incomprehensible to our thoughts, how can we comprehend with our mind Himself who hath prepared them?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: O the depth of the riches, and wisdom, and knowledge of God!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: How unsearchable are His judgments, and His ways past finding out! saith the Apostle.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If His judgments and His ways are incomprehensible, can He Himself be comprehended?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: God then being thus great, and yet greater, (for even were I to change my whole substance into tongue, I could not speak His excellence: nay more, not even if all Angels should assemble, could they ever speak His worth), God being therefore so great in goodness and majesty, man hath yet dared to say to a stone that he hath graven, Thou art my God!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: O monstrous blindness, that from majesty so great came down so low!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The tree which was planted by God, and nourished by the rain, and afterwards burnt and turned into ashes by the fire,—this is addressed as God, and the true God is despised.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But the wickedness of idolatry grew yet more prodigal, and cat, and dog, and wolf were worshipped instead of God: the man-eating lion also was worshipped instead of God, the most loving friend of man.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The snake and the serpent, counterfeit of him who thrust us out of Paradise, were worshipped, and He who planted Paradise was despised.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And I am ashamed to say, and yet do say it, even onions were worshipped among some.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Wine was given to make glad the heart of man: and Dionysus (Bacchus) was worshipped instead of God.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: God made corn by saying, Let the earth bring forth grass, yielding seed after his kind and after his likeness, that bread may strengthen man’s heart: why then was Demeter (Ceres) worshipped?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Fire cometh forth from striking stones together even to this day: how then was Hephæstus (Vulcan) the creator of fire?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Whence came the polytheistic error of the Greeks?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: God has no body: whence then the adulteries alleged among those who are by them called gods?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I say nothing of the transformations of Zeus into a swan: I am ashamed to speak of his transformations into a bull: for bellowings are unworthy of a god.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The god of the Greeks has been found an adulterer, yet are they not ashamed: for if he is an adulterer let him not be called a god.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: They tell also of deaths, and falls, and thunder-strokes of their gods.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Seest thou from how great a height and how low they have fallen?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Was it without reason then that the Son of God came down from heaven? or was it that He might heal so great a wound?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Was it without reason that the Son came? or was it in order that the Father might be acknowledged?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Thou hast learned what moved the Only-begotten to come down from the throne at God’s right hand.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The Father was despised, the Son must needs correct the error: for He Through Whom All Things Were Made must bring them all as offerings to the Lord of all.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The wound must be healed: for what could be worse than this disease, that a stone should be worshipped instead of God?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And not among the heathen only did the devil make these assaults; for many of those who are falsely called Christians, and wrongfully addressed by the sweet name of Christ, have ere now impiously dared to banish God from His own creation.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I mean the brood of heretics, those most ungodly men of evil name, pretending to be friends of Christ but utterly hating Him.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For he who blasphemes the Father of the Christ is an enemy of the Son.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: These men have dared to speak of two Godheads, one good and one evil!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: O monstrous blindness!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If a Godhead, then assuredly good.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But if not good, why called a Godhead?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For if goodness is an attribute of God; if loving-kindness, beneficence, almighty power, are proper to God, then of two things one, either in calling Him God let the name and operation be united; or if they would rob Him of His operations, let them not give Him the bare name.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Heretics have dared to say that there are two Gods, and of good and evil two sources, and these unbegotten.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If both are unbegotten it is certain that they are also equal, and both mighty.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: How then doth the light destroy the darkness?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And do they ever exist together, or are they separated?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Together they cannot be; for what fellowship hath light with darkness? saith the Apostle.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But if they are far from each other, it is certain that they hold also each his own place; and if they hold their own separate places, we are certainly in the realm of one God, and certainly worship one God.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For thus we must conclude, even if we assent to their folly, that we must worship one God.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Let us examine also what they say of the good God.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Hath He power or no power?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If He hath power, how did evil arise against His will?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And how doth the evil substance intrude, if He be not willing?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For if He knows but cannot hinder it, they charge Him with want of power; but if He has the power, yet hinders not, they accuse Him of treachery.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Mark too their want of sense.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: At one time they say that the Evil One hath no communion with the good God in the creation of the world; but at another time they say that he hath the fourth part only.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Also they say that the good God is the Father of Christ; but Christ they call this sun.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If, therefore according to them, the world was made by the Evil One, and the sun is in the world, how is the Son of the Good an unwilling slave in the kingdom of the Evil?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: We bemire ourselves in speaking of these things, but we do it lest any of those present should from ignorance fall into the mire of the heretics.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I know that I have defiled my own mouth and the ears of my listeners: yet it is expedient.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For it is much better to hear absurdities charged against others, than to fall into them from ignorance: far better that thou know the mire and hate it, than unawares fall into it.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For the godless system of the heresies is a road with many branches, and whenever a man has strayed from the one straight way, then he falls down precipices again and again.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The inventor of all heresy was Simon Magus: that Simon, who in the Acts of the Apostles thought to purchase with money the unsaleable grace of the Spirit, and heard the words, Thou hast neither part nor lot in this matter, and the rest: concerning whom also it is written, They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would have remained with us.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: This man, after he had been cast out by the Apostles, came to Rome, and gaining over one Helena a harlot, was the first that dared with blasphemous mouth to say that it was himself who appeared on Mount Sinai as the Father, and afterwards appeared among the Jews, not in real flesh but in seeming, as Christ Jesus, and afterwards as the Holy Spirit whom Christ promised to send as the Paraclete.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And he so deceived the City of Rome that Claudius set up his statue, and wrote beneath it, in the language of the Romans, “Simoni Deo Sancto,” which being interpreted signifies, “To Simon the Holy God.”

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: As the delusion was extending, Peter and Paul, a noble pair, chief rulers of the Church, arrived and set the error right; and when the supposed god Simon wished to shew himself off, they straightway shewed him as a corpse.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For Simon promised to rise aloft to heaven, and came riding in a dæmons’ chariot on the air; but the servants of God fell on their knees, and having shewn that agreement of which Jesus spake, that If two of you shall agree concerning anything that they shall ask, it shall be done unto them, they launched the weapon of their concord in prayer against Magus, and struck him down to the earth.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And marvellous though it was, yet no marvel.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For Peter was there, who carrieth the keys of heaven: and nothing wonderful, for Paul was there, who was caught up to the third heaven, and into Paradise, and heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful far a man to utter.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: These brought the supposed God down from the sky to earth, thence to be taken down to the regions below the earth.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: In this man first the serpent of wickedness appeared; but when one head had been cut off, the root of wickedness was found again with many heads.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For Cerinthus made havoc of the Church, and Menander, and Carpocrates, Ebionites also, and Marcion, that mouthpiece of ungodliness.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For he who proclaimed different gods, one the Good, the other the Just, contradicts the Son when He says, O righteous Father.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And he who says again that the Father is one, and the maker of the world another, opposes the Son when He says, If then God so clothes the grass of the field which to-day is, and to-morrow is cast into the furnace of fire; and, Who maketh His sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Here again is a second inventor of more mischief, this Marcion.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For being confuted by the testimonies from the Old Testament which are quoted in the New, he was the first who dared to cut those testimonies out, and leave the preaching of the word of faith without witness, thus effacing the true God: and sought to undermine the Church’s faith, as if there were no heralds of it.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He again was succeeded by another, Basilides, of evil name, and dangerous character, a preacher of impurities.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The contest of wickedness was aided also by Valentinus, a preacher of thirty gods.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The Greeks tell of but few: and the man who was called—but more truly was not—a Christian extended the delusion to full thirty.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He says, too, that Bythus the Abyss (for it became him as being an abyss of wickedness to begin his teaching from the Abyss) begat Silence, and of Silence begat the Word.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: This Bythus was worse than the Zeus of the Greeks, who was united to his sister: for Silence was said to be the child of Bythus.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Dost thou see the absurdity invested with a show of Christianity?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Wait a little, and thou wilt be shocked at his impiety; for he asserts that of this Bythus were begotten eight Æons; and of them, ten; and of them, other twelve, male and female.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But whence is the proof of these things?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: See their silliness from their fabrications.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Whence hast thou the proof of the thirty Æons?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Because, saith he, it is written, that Jesus was baptized, being thirty years old.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But even if He was baptized when thirty years old, what sort of demonstration is this from the thirty years?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Are there then five gods, because He brake five loaves among five thousand?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Or because he had twelve Disciples, must there also be twelve gods?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And even this is still little compared with the impieties which follow.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For the last of the deities being, as he dares to speak, both male and female, this, he says, is Wisdom.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: What impiety!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For the Wisdom of God is Christ His Only-begotten Son: and he by his doctrine degraded the Wisdom of God into a female element, and one of thirty, and the last fabrication.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He also says that Wisdom attempted to behold the first God, and not bearing His brightness fell from heaven, and was cast out of her thirtieth place.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Then she groaned, and of her groans begat the Devil, and as she wept over her fall made of her tears the sea.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Mark the impiety.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For of Wisdom how is the Devil begotten, and of prudence wickedness, or of light darkness?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He says too that the Devil begat others, some of whom created the world: and that the Christ came down in order to make mankind revolt from the Maker of the world.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But hear whom they say Christ Jesus to be, that thou mayest detest them yet more.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For they say that after Wisdom had been cast down, in order that the number of the thirty might not be incomplete, the nine and twenty Æons contributed each a little part, and formed the Christ: and they say that He also is both male and female.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Can anything be more impious than this?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Anything more wretched?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I am describing their delusion to thee, in order that thou mayest hate them the more.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Shun, therefore, their impiety, and do not even give greeting to a man of this kind, lest thou have fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness: neither make curious inquiries, nor be willing to enter into conversation with them.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Hate all heretics, but especially him who is rightly named after mania, who arose not long ago in the reign of Probus.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For the delusion began full seventy years ago, and there are men still living who saw him with their very eyes.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But hate him not for this, that he lived a short time ago; but because of his impious doctrines hate thou the worker of wickedness, the receptacle of all filth, who gathered up the mire of every heresy.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For aspiring to become pre-eminent among wicked men, he took the doctrines of all, and having combined them into one heresy filled with blasphemies and all iniquity, he makes havoc of the Church, or rather of those outside the Church, roaming about like a lion and devouring.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Heed not their fair speech, nor their supposed humility: for they are serpents, a generation of vipers.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Judas too said Hail!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Master, even while he was betraying Him.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Heed not their kisses, but beware of their venom.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Now, lest I seem to accuse him without reason, let me make a digression to tell who this Manes is, and in part what he teaches: for all time would fail to describe adequately the whole of his foul teaching.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But for help in time of need, store up in thy memory what I have said to former hearers, and will repeat to those now present, that they who know not may learn, and they who know may be reminded.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Manes is not of Christian origin, God forbid! nor was he like Simon cast out of the Church, neither himself nor the teachers who were before him.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For he steals other men’s wickedness, and makes their wickedness his own: but how and in what manner thou must hear.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: There was in Egypt one Scythianus, a Saracen by birth, having nothing in common either with Judaism or with Christianity.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: This man, who dwelt at Alexandria and imitated the life of Aristotle, composed four books, one called a Gospel which had not the acts of Christ, but the mere name only, and one other called the book of Chapters, and a third of Mysteries, and a fourth, which they circulate now, the Treasure.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: This man had a disciple, Terebinthus by name.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But when Scythianus purposed to come into Judæa, and make havoc of the land, the Lord smote him with a deadly disease, and stayed the pestilence.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But Terebinthus, his disciple in this wicked error, inherited his money and books and heresy, and came to Palestine, and becoming known and condemned in Judæa he resolved to pass into Persia: but lest he should be recognised there also by his name he changed it and called himself Buddas.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: However, he found adversaries there also in the priests of Mithras: and being confuted in the discussion of many arguments and controversies, and at last hard pressed, he took refuge with a certain widow.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Then having gone up on the housetop, and summoned the dæmons of the air, whom the Manichees to this day invoke over their abominable ceremony of the fig, he was smitten of God, and cast down from the housetop, and expired: and so the second beast was cut off.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The books, however, which were the records of his impiety, remained; and both these and his money the widow inherited.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And having neither kinsman nor any other friend, she determined to buy with the money a boy named Cubricus: him she adopted and educated as a son in the learning of the Persians, and thus sharpened an evil weapon against mankind.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: So Cubricus, the vile slave, grew up in the midst of philosophers, and on the death of the widow inherited both the books and the money.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Then, lest the name of slavery might be a reproach, instead of Cubricus he called himself Manes, which in the language of the Persians signifies discourse.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For as he thought himself something of a disputant, he surnamed himself Manes, as it were an excellent master of discourse.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But though he contrived for himself an honourable title according to the language of the Persians, yet the providence of God caused him to become a self-accuser even against his will, that through thinking to honour himself in Persia, he might proclaim himself among the Greeks by name a maniac.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He dared too to say that he was the Paraclete, though it is written, But whosoever shall blaspheme against the Holy Ghost, hath no forgiveness.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He committed blasphemy therefore by saying that he was the Holy Ghost: let him that communicates with those heretics see with whom he is enrolling himself.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The slave shook the world, since by three things the earth is shaken, and the fourth it cannot bear,—if a slave became a king.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Having come into public he now began to promise things above man’s power.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The son of the King of the Persians was sick, and a multitude of physicians were in attendance: but Manes promised, as if he were a godly man, to cure him by prayer.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: With the departure of the physicians, the life of the child departed: and the man’s impiety was detected.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: So the would-be philosopher was a prisoner, being cast into prison not for reproving the king in the cause of truth, not for destroying the idols, but for promising to save and lying, or rather, if the truth must be told, for committing murder.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For the child who might have been saved by medical treatment, was murdered by this man’s driving away the physicians, and killing him by want of treatment.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Now as there are very many wicked things which I tell thee of him, remember first his blasphemy, secondly his slavery (not that slavery is a disgrace, but that his pretending to be free-born, when he was a slave, was wicked), thirdly, the falsehood of his promise, fourthly, the murder of the child, and fifthly, the disgrace of the imprisonment.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And there was not only the disgrace of the prison, but also the flight from prison.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For he who called himself the Paraclete and champion of the truth, ran away: he was no successor of Jesus, who readily went to the Cross, but this man was the reverse, a runaway.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Moreover, the King of the Persians ordered the keepers of the prison to be executed: so Manes was the cause of the child’s death through his vain boasting, and of the gaolers’ death through his flight.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Ought then he, who shared the guilt of murder, to be worshipped?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Ought he not to have followed the example of Jesus, and said, If ye seek Me, let these go their way?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Ought he not to have said, like Jonas, Take me, and cast me into the sea: for this storm is because of me?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He escapes from the prison, and comes into Mesopotamia: but there Bishop Archelaus, a shield of righteousness, encounters him: and having accused him before philosophers as judges, and having assembled an audience of Gentiles, lest if Christians gave judgment, the judges might be thought to shew favour,—Tell us what thou preachest, said Archelaus to Manes.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And he, whose mouth was as an open sepulchre, began first with blasphemy against the Maker of all things, saying, The God of the Old Testament is the author of evils, as He says of Himself, I am a consuming fire.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But the wise Archelaus undermined his blasphemous argument by saying, “If the God of the Old Testament, as thou sayest, calls Himself a fire, whose Son is He who saith, I came to send fire on the earth?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If thou findest fault with Him who saith, The Lord killeth, and maketh alive, why dost thou honour Peter, who raised up Tabitha, but struck Sapphira dead?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If again thou findest fault, because He prepared fire, wherefore dost thou not find fault with Him who saith, Depart from Me into everlasting fire?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If thou findest fault with Him who saith, I am God that make peace, and create evil, explain how Jesus saith, I came not to send peace but a sword.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Since both speak alike, of two things one, either both are good, because of their agreement, or if Jesus is blameless in so speaking. why blamest thou Him that saith the like in the Old Testament?”

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Then Manes answers him: “And what sort of God causes blindness?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For it is Paul who saith, In whom the God of this world hath blinded the minds of them that believe not, lest the light of the Gospel should shine unto them.”

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But Archelaus made a good retort, saying, “Read a little before: But if our Gospel is veiled, it is veiled in them that are perishing.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Seest thou that in them that are perishing it is veiled?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For it is not right to give the things which are holy unto the dogs.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Again, Is it only the God of the Old Testament that hath blinded the minds of them that believe not?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Hath not Jesus Himself said, For this cause speak I unto them in parables, that seeing they may not see?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Was it from hating them that He wished them not to see?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Or because of their unworthiness, since their eyes they had closed.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For where there is wilful wickedness, there is also a withholding of grace: for to him that hath shall be given; but from him that hath not shall be taken even that which he seemeth to have.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: “But if some are right in their interpretation, we must say as follows (for it is no unworthy expression)—If indeed He blinded the thoughts of them that believe not he blinded them for a good purpose, that they might look with new sight on what is good.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For he said not, He blinded their soul, but, the thoughts of them that believe not.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And the meaning is something of this kind: ‘Blind the lewd thoughts of the lewd, and the man is saved: blind the grasping and rapacious thought of the robber, and the man is saved.’

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But wilt thou not understand it thus?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Then there is yet another interpretation.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The sun also blinds those whose sight is dim: and they whose eyes are diseased are hurt by the light and blinded.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Not that the sun’s nature is to blind, but that the substance of the eyes is incapable of seeing.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: In like manner unbelievers being diseased in their heart cannot look upon the radiance of the Godhead.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Nor hath he said, ‘He hath blinded their thoughts, that they should not hear the Gospel:’ but, that the light of the glory of the Gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ should not shine unto them.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For to hear the Gospel is permitted to all: but the glory of the Gospel is reserved for Christ’s true children only.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Therefore the Lord spoke in parables to those who could not hear: but to the Disciples he explained the parables in private: for the brightness of the glory is for those who have been enlightened, the blinding for them that believe not.”

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: These mysteries, which the Church now explains to thee who art passing out of the class of Catechumens, it is not the custom to explain to heathen.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For to a heathen we do not explain the mysteries concerning Father, Son, and Holy Ghost, nor before Catechumens do we speak plainly of the mysteries: but many things we often speak in a veiled way, that the believers who know may understand, and they who know not may get no hurt.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: By such and many other arguments the serpent was overthrown: thus did Archelaus wrestle with Manes and threw him.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Again, he who had fled from prison flees from this place also: and having run away from his antagonist, he comes to a very poor village, like the serpent in Paradise when he left Adam and came to Eve.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But the good shepherd Archelaus taking forethought for his sheep, when he heard of his flight, straightway hastened with all speed in search of the wolf.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And when Manes suddenly saw his adversary, he rushed out and fled: it was however his last flight.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For the officers of the King of Persia searched everywhere, and caught the fugitive: and the sentence, which he ought to have received in the presence of Archelaus, is passed upon him by the king’s officers.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: This Manes, whom his own disciples worship, is arrested and brought before the king.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The king reproached him with his falsehood and his flight: poured scorn upon his slavish condition, avenged the murder of his child, and condemned him also for the murder of the gaolers: he commands him to be flayed after the Persian fashion.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And while the rest of his body was given over for food of wild beasts, his skin, the receptacle of his vile mind, was hung up before the gates like a sack.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He that called himself the Paraclete and professed to know the future, knew not his own flight and capture.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: This man has had three disciples, Thomas, and Baddas, and Hermas.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Let none read the Gospel according to Thomas: for it is the work not of one of the twelve Apostles, but of one of the three wicked disciples of Manes.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Let none associate with the soul-destroying Manicheans, who by decoctions of chaff counterfeit the sad look of fasting, who speak evil of the Creator of meats, and greedily devour the daintiest, who teach that the man who plucks up this or that herb is changed into it.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For if he who crops herbs or any vegetable is changed into the same, into how many will husbandmen and the tribe of gardeners be changed?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The gardener, as we see, has used his sickle against so many: into which then is he changed?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Verily their doctrines are ridiculous, and fraught with their own condemnation and shame!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The same man, being the shepherd of a flock, both sacrifices a sheep and kills a wolf.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Into what then is he changed?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Many men both net fishes and lime birds: into which then are they transformed?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Let those children of sloth, the Manicheans, make answer; who without labouring themselves eat up the labourers’ fruits: who welcome with smiling faces those who bring them their food, and return curses instead of blessings.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For when a simple person brings them anything, “Stand outside a while,” saith he, “and I will bless thee.”

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Then having taken the bread into his hands (as those who have repented and left them have confessed), “I did not make thee,” says the Manichee to the bread: and sends up curses against the Most High; and curses him that made it, and so eats what was made.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If thou hatest the food, why didst thou look with smiling countenance on him that brought it to thee?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If thou art thankful to the bringer, why dost thou utter thy blasphemy to God, who created and made it?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: So again he says, “I sowed thee not: may he be sown who sowed thee!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I reaped thee not with a sickle: may he be reaped who reaped thee!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I baked thee not with fire: may he be baked who baked thee!”

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: A fine return for the kindness!

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: These are great faults, but still small in comparison with the rest.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Their Baptism I dare not describe before men and women.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: I dare not say what they distribute to their wretched communicants….Truly we pollute our mouth in speaking of these things.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Are the heathen more detestable than these?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Are the Samaritans more wretched?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Are Jews more impious?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Are fornicators more impure?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But the Manichee sets these offerings in the midst of the altar as he considers it.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And dost thou, O man, receive instruction from such a mouth?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: On meeting this man dost thou greet him at all with a kiss?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: To say nothing of his other impiety, dost thou not flee from the defilement, and from men worse than profligates, more detestable than any prostitute?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Of these things the Church admonishes and teaches thee, and touches mire, that thou mayest not be bemired: she tells of the wounds, that thou mayest not be wounded.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But for thee it is enough merely to know them: abstain from learning by experience.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: God thunders, and we all tremble; and they blaspheme.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: God lightens, and we all bow down to the earth; and they have their blasphemous sayings about the heavens.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: These things are written in the books of the Manichees.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: These things we ourselves have read, because we could not believe those who told of them: yes, for the sake of your salvation we have closely inquired into their perdition.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But may the Lord deliver us from such delusion: and may there be given to you a hatred against the serpent, that as they lie in wait for the heel, so you may trample on their head.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Remember ye what I say.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: What agreement can there be between our state and theirs?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: What communion hath light with darkness?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: What hath the majesty of the Church to do with the abomination of the Manichees?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Here is order, here is discipline, here is majesty, here is purity: here even to look upon a woman to lust after her is condemnation.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Here is marriage with sanctity, here steadfast continence, here virginity in honour like unto the Angels: here partaking of food with thanksgiving, here gratitude to the Creator of the world.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Here the Father of Christ is worshipped: here are taught fear and trembling before Him who sends the rain: here we ascribe glory to Him who makes the thunder and the lightning.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Make thou thy fold with the sheep: flee from the wolves: depart not from the Church.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Hate those also who have ever been suspected in such matters: and unless in time thou perceive their repentance, do not rashly trust thyself among them.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The truth of the Unity of God has been delivered to thee: learn to distinguish the pastures of doctrine.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Be an approved banker, holding fast that which is good, abstaining from every form of evil.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Or if thou hast ever been such as they, recognise and hate thy delusion.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For there is a way of salvation, if thou reject the vomit, if thou from thy heart detest it, if thou depart from them, not with thy lips only, but with thy soul also: if thou worship the Father of Christ, the God of the Law and the Prophets, if thou acknowledge the Good and the Just to be one and the same God.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And may He preserve you all, guarding you from falling or stumbling, stablished in the Faith, in Christ Jesus our Lord, to Whom be glory for ever and ever.
